message.content: Write an usage skill for QEMU running on Andes all CPUs.

content.text: Let me first explore the codebase to understand what Andes CPUs are available and how QEMU is configured.

input.prompt:
Search the codebase thoroughly for all Andes RISC-V CPU definitions. I need:

1. All CPU type names/models defined for Andes (look in target/riscv/ for cpu types, machine names, etc.)
2. Any Andes-specific machine definitions (look in hw/riscv/ for andes machine files)
3. How CPUs are registered (cpu_type strings, aliases)
4. Any documentation or help text about running Andes machines

Search for patterns like "andes", "ANDES", "Andes" in target/riscv/, hw/riscv/, and include/ directories. Also check for machine registration in hw/riscv/. Look for CPU model names like "a25", "a45", "ax25", "ax45", "ax65", "d25", "n25", etc.

input.description: Find QEMU run examples and configs
input.prompt:
Search the codebase for:

1. Any existing skills or documentation about running QEMU with Andes machines (look in .claude/ directory, docs/, README files)
2. Machine types registered for Andes - look for "TYPE_ANDES" or "andes" in hw/riscv/ machine registration code
3. Command-line examples or test scripts that show how to invoke qemu-system-riscv32 or qemu-system-riscv64 with Andes machines
4. Any semihosting configuration examples
5. Look for files like CLAUDE.md, .claude/skills/, or any skill definitions

Search broadly across the repo.

   147→static void create_fdt_iopmp(AndesAe350BoardState *bs,
   148→                             const struct MemmapEntry *memmap,
   149→                             uint32_t irq_mmio_phandle)
   150→{
   151→    g_autofree char *name = NULL;
   152→    MachineState *ms = MACHINE(bs);

   154→
   155→    if (bs->soc.has_iopmp300) {
   156→        for (int i = 0; i < AE350_IOPMP300_NUM; i++) {
   157→            entry = &memmap[iopmp300_memmap_idx_list[i]];

   159→            qemu_fdt_add_subnode(ms->fdt, name);
   160→            qemu_fdt_setprop_string(ms->fdt, name, "compatible", "atciopmp300");
   161→            qemu_fdt_setprop_cells(ms->fdt, name, "reg", 0x0, entry->base,
   162→                                   0x0, entry->size);
   163→            qemu_fdt_setprop_cell(ms->fdt, name, "interrupt-parent",
   164→                                  irq_mmio_phandle);
   165→            qemu_fdt_setprop_cells(ms->fdt, name, "interrupts",
   166→                                   ANDES_AE350_IOPMP_IRQ, 0x4);
   167→        }
   168→    } else if (bs->soc.has_iopmp200) {
   169→        for (int i = 0; i < AE350_IOPMP200_NUM; i++) {
   170→            entry = &memmap[iopmp200_memmap_idx_list[i]];
   171→            name = g_strdup_printf("/soc/atciopmp200@%lx", (long)entry->base);
   172→            qemu_fdt_add_subnode(ms->fdt, name);
   173→            qemu_fdt_setprop_string(ms->fdt, name, "compatible", "atciopmp200");
   174→            qemu_fdt_setprop_cells(ms->fdt, name, "reg", 0x0, entry->base,

   176→            qemu_fdt_setprop_cell(ms->fdt, name, "interrupt-parent",

   178→            qemu_fdt_setprop_cells(ms->fdt, name, "interrupts",
   179→                                   ANDES_AE350_IOPMP_IRQ, 0x4);
   180→        }
   181→    }
   182→    if (bs->soc.has_iopmp100) {
   183→        entry = &memmap[ANDES_AE350_IOPMP100];
   184→        name = g_strdup_printf("/soc/atciopmp100@%lx", (long)entry->base);

1→======================
     2→Andes QEMU Quick Start
     3→======================
     4→
     5→| This document provides a default example for building and using Andes QEMU.
     6→| For more details, please refer to the `QEMU's documentation <https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/index.html>`_.
     7→
     8→
     9→Get the source
    10→==============
    11→
    12→Clone the Andes QEMU repository:
    13→
    14→.. code-block:: bash
    15→
    16→    git clone https://github.com/andestech/qemu.git
    17→
    18→
    19→Install the required dependencies
    20→=================================
    21→
    22→For Ubuntu 22.04 or later:
    23→
    24→.. code-block:: bash
    25→
    26→    sudo apt-get install git python3-virtualenv libglib2.0-dev libfdt-dev ninja-build
    27→
    28→If your python version is 3.10 or earlier, please install tomli:
    29→
    30→.. code-block:: bash
    31→
    32→    pip3 install --user tomli
    33→
    34→
    35→Build in Ubuntu
    36→===============
    37→
    38→Navigate to the cloned qemu directory and create a build directory:
    39→
    40→.. code-block:: bash
    41→
    42→    cd qemu
    43→    mkdir build
    44→    cd build
    45→
    46→Configure the build options:
    47→
    48→.. code-block:: bash
    49→
    50→    ../configure --target-list=riscv64-softmmu,riscv32-softmmu --without-default-features --enable-avx2 --enable-avx512bw --enable-vhost-net --enable-vhost-kernel --enable-vhost-user --enable-multiprocess --enable-slirp
    51→
    52→Build QEMU:
    53→
    54→.. code-block:: bash
    55→
    56→    ninja
    57→
    58→| After successful building, you should have the binaries :code:`qemu-system-riscv64` and :code:`qemu-system-riscv32`.
    59→| For more detailed information about building, refer to the `QEMU on Linux hosts <https://wiki.qemu.org/Hosts/Linux>`_.
    60→
    61→
    62→QEMU Options
    63→============
    64→
    65→=================   ==================================================
    66→   Option               Description
    67→=================   ==================================================
    68→-nographic          Direct the output to the terminal.
    69→-M andes_ae350      Use Andes AE350 platform.
    70→-cpu andes_ax45     Use Andes core AX45 for RV64.
    71→-m 2G               Set system memory to 2G(maximum in AE350 platform).
    72→-bios *elf_file*    Run the elf file.
    73→=================   ==================================================
    74→
    75→Run a demo
    76→==========
    77→
    78→| To run a simple printf/scanf demo, first compile the demo.
    79→| Please refer to the **Andes BSP User Manual** for instructions.
    80→|
    81→| Then execute the following command:
    82→
    83→.. code-block:: bash
    84→
    85→    qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    86→                        -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
    87→                        -m 2G -bios demo-printf-V5/CmdMakefile/Demo.elf
    88→
    89→
    90→Contact
    91→=======
    92→
    93→If you encounter any issues or problems, please contact nephih@andestech.com
    94→for assistance.

1→.. _RISC-V-System-emulator:
     2→
     3→RISC-V System emulator
     4→======================
     5→
     6→QEMU can emulate both 32-bit and 64-bit RISC-V CPUs. Use the
     7→``qemu-system-riscv64`` executable to simulate a 64-bit RISC-V machine,
     8→``qemu-system-riscv32`` executable to simulate a 32-bit RISC-V machine.
     9→
    10→QEMU has generally good support for RISC-V guests. It has support for
    11→several different machines. The reason we support so many is that
    12→RISC-V hardware is much more widely varying than x86 hardware. RISC-V
    13→CPUs are generally built into "system-on-chip" (SoC) designs created by
    14→many different companies with different devices, and these SoCs are
    15→then built into machines which can vary still further even if they use
    16→the same SoC.
    17→
    18→For most boards the CPU type is fixed (matching what the hardware has),
    19→so typically you don't need to specify the CPU type by hand, except for
    20→special cases like the ``virt`` board.
    21→
    22→Choosing a board model
    23→----------------------
    24→
    25→For QEMU's RISC-V system emulation, you must specify which board
    26→model you want to use with the ``-M`` or ``--machine`` option;
    27→there is no default.
    28→
    29→Because RISC-V systems differ so much and in fundamental ways, typically
    30→operating system or firmware images intended to run on one machine
    31→will not run at all on any other. This is often surprising for new
    32→users who are used to the x86 world where every system looks like a
    33→standard PC. (Once the kernel has booted, most user space software
    34→cares much less about the detail of the hardware.)
    35→
    36→If you already have a system image or a kernel that works on hardware
    37→and you want to boot with QEMU, check whether QEMU lists that machine
    38→in its ``-machine help`` output. If it is listed, then you can probably
    39→use that board model. If it is not listed, then unfortunately your image
    40→will almost certainly not boot on QEMU. (You might be able to
    41→extract the file system and use that with a different kernel which
    42→boots on a system that QEMU does emulate.)
    43→
    44→If you don't care about reproducing the idiosyncrasies of a particular
    45→bit of hardware, such as small amount of RAM, no PCI or other hard
    46→disk, etc., and just want to run Linux, the best option is to use the
    47→``virt`` board. This is a platform which doesn't correspond to any
    48→real hardware and is designed for use in virtual machines. You'll
    49→need to compile Linux with a suitable configuration for running on
    50→the ``virt`` board. ``virt`` supports PCI, virtio, recent CPUs and
    51→large amounts of RAM. It also supports 64-bit CPUs.
    52→
    53→Board-specific documentation
    54→----------------------------
    55→
    56→Unfortunately many of the RISC-V boards QEMU supports are currently
    57→undocumented; you can get a complete list by running
    58→``qemu-system-riscv64 --machine help``, or
    59→``qemu-system-riscv32 --machine help``.
    60→
    61→..
    62→   This table of contents should be kept sorted alphabetically
    63→   by the title text of each file, which isn't the same ordering
    64→   as an alphabetical sort by filename.
    65→
    66→.. toctree::
    67→   :maxdepth: 1
    68→
    69→   riscv/microchip-icicle-kit
    70→   riscv/shakti-c
    71→   riscv/sifive_u
    72→   riscv/virt
    73→
    74→RISC-V CPU firmware
    75→-------------------
    76→
    77→When using the ``sifive_u`` or ``virt`` machine there are three different
    78→firmware boot options:
    79→
    80→* ``-bios default``
    81→
    82→This is the default behaviour if no ``-bios`` option is included. This option
    83→will load the default OpenSBI firmware automatically. The firmware is included
    84→with the QEMU release and no user interaction is required. All a user needs to
    85→do is specify the kernel they want to boot with the ``-kernel`` option
    86→
    87→* ``-bios none``
    88→
    89→QEMU will not automatically load any firmware. It is up to the user to load all
    90→the images they need.
    91→
    92→* ``-bios <file>``
    93→
    94→Tells QEMU to load the specified file as the firmware.
    95→

Based on my comprehensive search of the Andes QEMU codebase, here are the key findings:

- `/local/nick/qemu_andes/docs/system/target-riscv.rst` - RISC-V system emulator documentation

### 2. Machine Types Registered for Andes

**Machine Type:**
- Machine name: `andes_ae350` (registered as `MACHINE_TYPE_NAME("andes_ae350")`)
- File: `/local/nick/qemu_andes/hw/riscv/andes_ae350.c`

- Description: "RISC-V Board compatible with Andes AE350"
- Maximum CPUs: 8 (`ANDES_CPUS_MAX`)

**SoC Type:**
- Type constant: `TYPE_ANDES_AE350_SOC` = `"riscv.andes.ae350.soc"`
- File: `/local/nick/qemu_andes/hw/riscv/andes_ae350.c`

### 3. Supported Andes CPU Types

The following Andes CPU types are registered in `/local/nick/qemu_andes/target/riscv/cpu-qom.h`:

**32-bit RISC-V (RV32) CPUs:**
- `andes-a25`, `andes-a27`, `andes-a45`, `andes-a46mp`, `andes-a46mpv`
- `andes-d23`, `andes-d25`, `andes-d25f`, `andes-d45`
- `andes-n25`, `andes-n25f`, `andes-n25f-se`, `andes-n225`, `andes-n45`
- `andes-nx25`, `andes-nx25f`, `andes-nx27v`, `andes-nx45`, `andes-nx45v`

**64-bit RISC-V (RV64) CPUs:**
- `andes-ax25`, `andes-ax27`, `andes-ax45`, `andes-ax45mpv`
- `andes-ax46mp`, `andes-ax46mpv`, `andes-ax65`, `andes-ax66`

### 4. Command-Line Examples

**From QUICKSTART.rst - Basic Example:**
```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
                    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
                    -m 2G -bios demo-printf-V5/CmdMakefile/Demo.elf
```

**From docs/config/andes.cfg - Using readconfig:**
```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 \
  -M andes_ae350 -cpu [andes-nx45v|...]
  -readconfig andes.cfg
```

**Key Options:**
- `-nographic` - Direct output to terminal
- `-M andes_ae350` - Use Andes AE350 platform
- `-cpu <cpu_type>` - Specify CPU type (e.g., `andes-ax45`, `andes-nx45v`)
- `-m 2G` - Set system memory to 2GB (maximum in AE350 platform)
- `-bios <elf_file>` - Run the elf file

### 5. Semihosting Configuration

**Semihosting Support:** Andes Virtual Hosting (VH) is supported for semihosting calls.

**File:** `/local/nick/qemu_andes/semihosting/guestfd.c`

Key features:
- Pre-mapped guest file descriptors 0/1/2 (stdin/stdout/stderr) to host file descriptors for Andes Virtual Hosting (VH)
- Support for `SYS_WRITE`, `SYS_FLEN`, and other file operations
- ARM-compatible semihosting configuration available

### 6. Memory Map Information

From `/local/nick/qemu_andes/include/hw/riscv/andes_ae350.h`:

**Key Memory Regions:**
- **DRAM:** 0x00000000 - 0x80000000 (2GB)
- **MROM (Boot ROM):** 0x80000000 - 0x88000000
- **NOR Flash:** 0x88000000 - 0x8C000000
- **ILM/DLM:** 0xA0000000 - 0xA2000000 (per-core instruction/data local memory)
- **Interrupt Controllers:**
  - **PLIC:** 0xE4000000 - 0xE4400000
  - **APLIC:** 0xE4400000 - 0xE4500000
  - **PLICSW:** Part of PLIC range
  - **IMSIC_M:** 0xC4000000 - 0xC4100000
  - **IMSIC_S:** 0xC4100000 - 0xC4200000

### 7. Key Andes-Specific Features

**Local Memory Configuration** (via properties):
- Instruction Local Memory (ILM): configurable base and size
- Data Local Memory (DLM): configurable base and size (default: 0x200000, size: 0x200000)
- Uncacheable alias enable option

**BMC Type Selection:**
- `axi` - AXI Bus Matrix Controller
- `ahb` - AHB Bus Matrix Controller (default)

**Interrupt Controller Support:**
- Legacy PLIC (default)
- APLIC only
- APLIC with IMSIC (for AIA - Advanced Interrupt Architecture)

**Security Features:**
- Optional Instruction Operation Memory Protection (IOPMP) configurations
- Secure platform support (none/atciopmp300/atciopmp200/atciopmp100)

### 8. Important Files and Paths

- **Machine definition:** `/local/nick/qemu_andes/hw/riscv/andes_ae350.c` (27,000+ lines)
- **Header file:** `/local/nick/qemu_andes/include/hw/riscv/andes_ae350.h`
- **CPU definitions:** `/local/nick/qemu_andes/target/riscv/cpu-qom.h`
- **Configuration:** `/local/nick/qemu_andes/docs/config/andes.cfg`
- **Quick start guide:** `/local/nick/qemu_andes/QUICKSTART.rst`

1→======================
     2→Andes QEMU Quick Start
     3→======================
     4→
     5→| This document provides a default example for building and using Andes QEMU.
     6→| For more details, please refer to the `QEMU's documentation <https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/index.html>`_.
     7→
     8→
     9→Get the source
    10→==============
    11→
    12→Clone the Andes QEMU repository:
    13→
    14→.. code-block:: bash
    15→
    16→    git clone https://github.com/andestech/qemu.git
    17→
    18→
    19→Install the required dependencies
    20→=================================
    21→
    22→For Ubuntu 22.04 or later:
    23→
    24→.. code-block:: bash
    25→
    26→    sudo apt-get install git python3-virtualenv libglib2.0-dev libfdt-dev ninja-build
    27→
    28→If your python version is 3.10 or earlier, please install tomli:
    29→
    30→.. code-block:: bash
    31→
    32→    pip3 install --user tomli
    33→
    34→
    35→Build in Ubuntu
    36→===============
    37→
    38→Navigate to the cloned qemu directory and create a build directory:
    39→
    40→.. code-block:: bash
    41→
    42→    cd qemu
    43→    mkdir build
    44→    cd build
    45→
    46→Configure the build options:
    47→
    48→.. code-block:: bash
    49→
    50→    ../configure --target-list=riscv64-softmmu,riscv32-softmmu --without-default-features --enable-avx2 --enable-avx512bw --enable-vhost-net --enable-vhost-kernel --enable-vhost-user --enable-multiprocess --enable-slirp
    51→
    52→Build QEMU:
    53→
    54→.. code-block:: bash
    55→
    56→    ninja
    57→
    58→| After successful building, you should have the binaries :code:`qemu-system-riscv64` and :code:`qemu-system-riscv32`.
    59→| For more detailed information about building, refer to the `QEMU on Linux hosts <https://wiki.qemu.org/Hosts/Linux>`_.
    60→
    61→
    62→QEMU Options
    63→============
    64→
    65→=================   ==================================================
    66→   Option               Description
    67→=================   ==================================================
    68→-nographic          Direct the output to the terminal.
    69→-M andes_ae350      Use Andes AE350 platform.
    70→-cpu andes_ax45     Use Andes core AX45 for RV64.
    71→-m 2G               Set system memory to 2G(maximum in AE350 platform).
    72→-bios *elf_file*    Run the elf file.
    73→=================   ==================================================
    74→
    75→Run a demo
    76→==========
    77→
    78→| To run a simple printf/scanf demo, first compile the demo.
    79→| Please refer to the **Andes BSP User Manual** for instructions.
    80→|
    81→| Then execute the following command:
    82→
    83→.. code-block:: bash
    84→
    85→    qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    86→                        -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
    87→                        -m 2G -bios demo-printf-V5/CmdMakefile/Demo.elf
    88→
    89→
    90→Contact
    91→=======
    92→
    93→If you encounter any issues or problems, please contact nephih@andestech.com
    94→for assistance.

======================

======================

| This document provides a default example for building and using Andes QEMU.
| For more details, please refer to the `QEMU's documentation <https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/index.html>`_.


Get the source
==============

Clone the Andes QEMU repository:

.. code-block:: bash

    git clone https://github.com/andestech/qemu.git


Install the required dependencies
=================================

For Ubuntu 22.04 or later:

.. code-block:: bash

    sudo apt-get install git python3-virtualenv libglib2.0-dev libfdt-dev ninja-build

If your python version is 3.10 or earlier, please install tomli:

.. code-block:: bash

    pip3 install --user tomli


Build in Ubuntu
===============

Navigate to the cloned qemu directory and create a build directory:

.. code-block:: bash

    cd qemu
    mkdir build
    cd build

Configure the build options:

.. code-block:: bash

    ../configure --target-list=riscv64-softmmu,riscv32-softmmu --without-default-features --enable-avx2 --enable-avx512bw --enable-vhost-net --enable-vhost-kernel --enable-vhost-user --enable-multiprocess --enable-slirp

Build QEMU:

.. code-block:: bash

    ninja

| After successful building, you should have the binaries :code:`qemu-system-riscv64` and :code:`qemu-system-riscv32`.
| For more detailed information about building, refer to the `QEMU on Linux hosts <https://wiki.qemu.org/Hosts/Linux>`_.


QEMU Options
============

=================   ==================================================
   Option               Description
=================   ==================================================
-nographic          Direct the output to the terminal.
-M andes_ae350      Use Andes AE350 platform.
-cpu andes_ax45     Use Andes core AX45 for RV64.
-m 2G               Set system memory to 2G(maximum in AE350 platform).
-bios *elf_file*    Run the elf file.
=================   ==================================================

Run a demo
==========

| To run a simple printf/scanf demo, first compile the demo.
| Please refer to the **Andes BSP User Manual** for instructions.
|
| Then execute the following command:

.. code-block:: bash

    qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
                        -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
                        -m 2G -bios demo-printf-V5/CmdMakefile/Demo.elf


Contact
=======

If you encounter any issues or problems, please contact nephih@andestech.com
for assistance.

# Andes QEMU Usage Guide

## Skill Description

Provide usage examples for running QEMU with all Andes RISC-V CPUs on the `andes_ae350` machine platform.

## Instructions

When the user asks about QEMU usage for Andes CPUs, provide the relevant examples below. Tailor the response based on whether they need RV32 or RV64, a specific CPU model, or a general overview.

## Machine

The only Andes machine is `andes_ae350` (Andes AE350 platform). It supports up to 8 CPUs and a maximum of 2GB DRAM.

## All Andes CPU Models

### RV32 (use `qemu-system-riscv32`)

| CPU (`-cpu`)    | Series | Notes                        |
|-----------------|--------|------------------------------|
| andes-n25       | N25    | Base integer, no FPU         |
| andes-n25f      | N25F   | With single/double FPU       |
| andes-n25f-se   | N25F   | With security extensions      |
| andes-n225      | N225   | Low-power variant            |
| andes-n45       | N45    | Superscalar, no FPU          |
| andes-d23       | D23    | DSP-capable                  |
| andes-d25       | D25    | DSP + base integer           |
| andes-d25f      | D25F   | DSP + FPU                    |
| andes-d45       | D45    | DSP superscalar              |
| andes-a25       | A25    | Linux-capable (MMU)          |
| andes-a27       | A27    | Linux-capable + vector       |
| andes-a45       | A45    | Linux-capable superscalar    |
| andes-a46mp     | A46MP  | Multiprocessor               |
| andes-a46mpv    | A46MPV | Multiprocessor + vector      |

### RV64 (use `qemu-system-riscv64`)

| CPU (`-cpu`)     | Series  | Notes                       |
|------------------|---------|-----------------------------|
| andes-nx25       | NX25    | Base integer, no FPU        |
| andes-nx25f      | NX25F   | With single/double FPU      |
| andes-nx27v      | NX27V   | With vector extension        |
| andes-nx45       | NX45    | Superscalar, no FPU         |
| andes-nx45v      | NX45V   | Superscalar + vector        |
| andes-ax25       | AX25    | Linux-capable (MMU)         |
| andes-ax27       | AX27    | Linux-capable + vector      |
| andes-ax45       | AX45    | Linux-capable superscalar   |
| andes-ax45mpv    | AX45MPV | Multiprocessor + vector     |
| andes-ax46mp     | AX46MP  | Multiprocessor              |
| andes-ax46mpv    | AX46MPV | Multiprocessor + vector     |
| andes-ax65       | AX65    | High-performance            |
| andes-ax66       | AX66    | High-performance next-gen   |

### CPU Naming Convention

- **N/NX**: No MMU (bare-metal / RTOS)
- **D**: DSP extensions (RV32 only)
- **A/AX**: With MMU (Linux-capable)
- **25/45/65/66**: Microarchitecture generation (45 = superscalar, 65/66 = high-perf)
- **F** suffix: hardware FPU
- **V** suffix: vector extension
- **MP** suffix: multiprocessor support

## Basic Usage

### Minimal bare-metal run (RV64)

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### Minimal bare-metal run (RV32)

```bash
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a25 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### Multi-core run (up to 8 CPUs)

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 -smp 4 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### Using a configuration file

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45v \
    -readconfig docs/config/andes.cfg
```

## Common Options

| Option                | Description                                       |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| `-nographic`          | Direct output to terminal (no GUI window)         |
| `-M andes_ae350`      | Select Andes AE350 platform                       |
| `-cpu <model>`        | Select CPU model (see tables above)               |
| `-m 2G`               | Set memory size (max 2G for AE350)                |
| `-smp N`              | Number of CPU cores (max 8)                       |
| `-bios <elf>`         | Load and run an ELF binary                        |
| `-readconfig <cfg>`   | Load machine configuration from file              |
| `-d <items>`          | Enable debug logging (e.g., `int,cpu,in_asm`)     |
| `-D <logfile>`        | Write debug log to file                           |
| `-S`                  | Freeze CPU at startup (use with `-gdb`)           |
| `-gdb tcp::<port>`    | Start GDB server on given port                    |
| `-s`                  | Shorthand for `-gdb tcp::1234`                    |

## Advanced Options

### GDB debugging

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf \
    -s -S
```

Then connect with GDB:

```bash
riscv64-unknown-elf-gdb firmware.elf -ex "target remote :1234"
```

### ILM/DLM local memory configuration

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf \
    -global driver=riscv.andes.ae350.soc,property=ilm_default_enable,value=on \
    -global driver=riscv.andes.ae350.soc,property=ilm_size,value=0x10000
```

### ACE (Andes Custom Extension) library

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45v,xandesaceextlibpath=path/to/lib \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### AIA (Advanced Interrupt Architecture) with APLIC+IMSIC

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350,aia=aplic-imsic -cpu andes-ax45 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

## Run Examples for Every CPU

### RV32 CPUs

```bash
# N-series (no MMU, bare-metal/RTOS)
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n25f -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n25f-se -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n225 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf

# D-series (DSP)
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d23 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d25f -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf

# A-series (Linux-capable, with MMU)
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a27 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a46mp -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a46mpv -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### RV64 CPUs

```bash
# NX-series (no MMU, bare-metal/RTOS)
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx25f -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx27v -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45v -m 2G -bios firmware.elf

# AX-series (Linux-capable, with MMU)
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax27 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax46mp -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax46mpv -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax65 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax66 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

| andes-n25f-se   | N25F   | With security extensions      |
| andes-n225      | N225   | Low-power variant            |
| andes-n45       | N45    | Superscalar, no FPU          |
| andes-d23       | D23    | DSP-capable                  |
| andes-d25       | D25    | DSP + base integer           |
| andes-d25f      | D25F   | DSP + FPU                    |
| andes-d45       | D45    | DSP superscalar              |
| andes-a25       | A25    | Linux-capable (MMU)          |
| andes-a27       | A27    | Linux-capable + vector       |
| andes-a45       | A45    | Linux-capable superscalar    |
| andes-a46mp     | A46MP  | Multiprocessor               |
| andes-a46mpv    | A46MPV | Multiprocessor + vector      |

### RV64 (use `qemu-system-riscv64`)

| CPU (`-cpu`)     | Series  | Notes                       |
|------------------|---------|-----------------------------|
| andes-nx25       | NX25    | Base integer, no FPU        |
| andes-nx25f      | NX25F   | With single/double FPU      |
| andes-nx27v      | NX27V   | With vector extension        |
| andes-nx45       | NX45    | Superscalar, no FPU         |
| andes-nx45v      | NX45V   | Superscalar + vector        |
| andes-ax25       | AX25    | Linux-capable (MMU)         |
| andes-ax27       | AX27    | Linux-capable + vector      |
| andes-ax45       | AX45    | Linux-capable superscalar   |
| andes-ax45mpv    | AX45MPV | Multiprocessor + vector     |
| andes-ax46mp     | AX46MP  | Multiprocessor              |
| andes-ax46mpv    | AX46MPV | Multiprocessor + vector     |
| andes-ax65       | AX65    | High-performance            |
| andes-ax66       | AX66    | High-performance next-gen   |

### CPU Naming Convention

- **N/NX**: No MMU (bare-metal / RTOS)
- **D**: DSP extensions (RV32 only)
- **A/AX**: With MMU (Linux-capable)
- **25/45/65/66**: Microarchitecture generation (45 = superscalar, 65/66 = high-perf)
- **F** suffix: hardware FPU
- **V** suffix: vector extension
- **MP** suffix: multiprocessor support

## Basic Usage

### Minimal bare-metal run (RV64)

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### Minimal bare-metal run (RV32)

```bash
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a25 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### Multi-core run (up to 8 CPUs)

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 -smp 4 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### Using a configuration file

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45v \
    -readconfig docs/config/andes.cfg
```

## Common Options

| Option                | Description                                       |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| `-nographic`          | Direct output to terminal (no GUI window)         |
| `-M andes_ae350`      | Select Andes AE350 platform                       |
| `-cpu <model>`        | Select CPU model (see tables above)               |
| `-m 2G`               | Set memory size (max 2G for AE350)                |
| `-smp N`              | Number of CPU cores (max 8)                       |
| `-bios <elf>`         | Load and run an ELF binary                        |
| `-readconfig <cfg>`   | Load machine configuration from file              |
| `-d <items>`          | Enable debug logging (e.g., `int,cpu,in_asm`)     |
| `-D <logfile>`        | Write debug log to file                           |
| `-S`                  | Freeze CPU at startup (use with `-gdb`)           |
| `-gdb tcp::<port>`    | Start GDB server on given port                    |
| `-s`                  | Shorthand for `-gdb tcp::1234`                    |

## Advanced Options

### GDB debugging

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf \
    -s -S
```

Then connect with GDB:

```bash
riscv64-unknown-elf-gdb firmware.elf -ex "target remote :1234"
```

### ILM/DLM local memory configuration

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf \
    -global driver=riscv.andes.ae350.soc,property=ilm_default_enable,value=on \
    -global driver=riscv.andes.ae350.soc,property=ilm_size,value=0x10000
```

### ACE (Andes Custom Extension) library

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45v,xandesaceextlibpath=path/to/lib \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### AIA (Advanced Interrupt Architecture) with APLIC+IMSIC

```bash
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic \
    -M andes_ae350,aia=aplic-imsic -cpu andes-ax45 \
    -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

## Run Examples for Every CPU

### RV32 CPUs

```bash
# N-series (no MMU, bare-metal/RTOS)
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf

qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n225 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-n45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf

# D-series (DSP)
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d23 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d25f -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-d45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf

# A-series (Linux-capable, with MMU)
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a27 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a46mp -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv32 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-a46mpv -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```

### RV64 CPUs

```bash
# NX-series (no MMU, bare-metal/RTOS)
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx25f -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx27v -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-nx45v -m 2G -bios firmware.elf

# AX-series (Linux-capable, with MMU)
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax25 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax27 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45 -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
qemu-system-riscv64 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -cpu andes-ax45mpv -m 2G -bios firmware.elf
```
